Straightforward, not evasive, candid and direct.
Frank, outspoken.
Markedly simple.
Fixed, settled, decided.
Proceeding straight forth.

example: He was always forthright about the company's challenges, even when the news was unwelcome — which is precisely why people trusted him.

definitions: Happening by chance; coincidental, accidental.
Happening by a lucky chance; lucky or fortunate.
Happening independently of human will.

example: Running into her former professor at the airport proved fortuitous — that chance encounter led directly to the collaboration that defined her career.

definitions: Avoiding unnecessary expenditure either of money or of anything else which is to be used or consumed; avoiding waste.

example: Her frugal habits — packing lunch, buying secondhand, shopping only from a list — allowed her to save more than most people earning twice her salary.

definitions: To coat with a thin layer of metal by electrochemical means.
To coat with rust-resistant zinc.
To shock or stimulate into sudden activity, as if by electric shock.
To electrify.
To switch sides between Union and Confederate in the American Civil War.

example: The documentary galvanized the public, transforming a slow-moving environmental concern into an urgent national conversation.

definitions: Excessively or tiresomely talkative.
(of something written or performed) Excessively wordy and rambling.

example: The garrulous cab driver offered an unbroken commentary on the city, its politics, and its history for the entire forty-minute ride.

definitions: (of a person) Describing one who enjoys being in crowds and socializing.
Of animals that travel in herds or packs.

example: Naturally gregarious, she could walk into a room full of strangers and leave an hour later with a dozen new acquaintances.

definitions: To make uninteresting or trite by frequent use.
To use as a hackney.
To carry in a hackney coach.
Repeated too often.
Let out for hire.

example: The script was full of hackneyed phrases — clichés so worn smooth that the audience could predict the next line before it was spoken.

definitions: A person or thing that foreshadows or foretells the coming of someone or something.
One who provides lodgings; especially, the officer of the English royal household who formerly preceded the court when travelling, to provide and prepare lodgings.
To announce or precede; to be a harbinger of.

example: The sudden departure of three senior executives was widely seen as a harbinger of deeper problems to come.

definitions: Careful attention.
To guard, protect.
To mind; to regard with care; to take notice of; to attend to; to observe.
To pay attention, care.

example: Had they heeded the early warning signs — the minor tremors, the rising groundwater — many casualties might have been avoided.

definitions: Of the same kind; alike, similar.
Having the same composition throughout; of uniform make-up.
In the same state of matter.
Of which the properties of a smaller set apply to the whole; scalable.

example: The neighbourhood had once been homogeneous in character, but over two decades it had become one of the most diverse communities in the city.

definitions: One who destroys religious images or icons, especially an opponent of the Orthodox Church in the 8th and 9th centuries, or a Puritan during the European Reformation.
One who opposes orthodoxy and religion; one who adheres to the doctrine of iconoclasm.
(by extension) One who attacks cherished beliefs.

example: An uncompromising iconoclast, she spent her career challenging assumptions that everyone else in her field had long accepted as settled.

definitions: Peculiar to a specific individual; eccentric.

example: His idiosyncratic filing system — organised by colour and intuition rather than any recognisable logic — somehow worked perfectly for him.

definitions: About to happen, occur, or take place very soon, especially of something which won't last long.

example: With dark clouds massing on the horizon and the barometric pressure dropping rapidly, a storm was clearly imminent.

definitions: Something that cannot be changed
Unable to be changed without exception.
(of a variable) not able to be altered in the memory after its value is set initially.

example: The laws of thermodynamics are immutable — no amount of engineering ingenuity can change them, only work within their constraints.

definitions: Treating all parties, rivals, or disputants equally; not partial; not biased

example: The judge was scrupulously impartial, applying the same standard to both sides regardless of public pressure or media attention.

definitions: (grammar) The grammatical mood expressing an order (see jussive). In English, the imperative form of a verb is the same as that of the bare infinitive.
(grammar) A verb in imperative mood.
An essential action, a must: something which is imperative.
Essential; crucial; extremely important.
(grammar) Of, or relating to the imperative mood.
Having semantics that incorporates mutable variables.
Expressing a command; authoritatively or absolutely directive.

example: With supplies running low and weather closing in fast, finding shelter before nightfall was absolutely imperative.

definitions: Lacking money

example: Despite his impecunious circumstances — sharing a single room, eating once a day — he refused every offer of financial help.

definitions: An impertinent individual.
Insolent, ill-mannered
Irrelevant (opposite of pertinent)

example: The reporter's impertinent questions, designed to provoke rather than inform, drew sharp rebukes from every member of the panel.

definitions: Making arbitrary decisions, especially in an impulsive and forceful manner.
Characterized by sudden violence or vehemence.

example: Her impetuous decision to quit her job and move abroad without a plan led to three years of adventure and one spectacular failure.

definitions: Implied indirectly, without being directly expressed
Contained in the essential nature of something but not openly shown
Having no reservations or doubts; unquestioning or unconditional; usually said of faith or trust.
Entangled, twisted together.

example: There was an implicit understanding that certain topics — the estrangement, the years of silence — would not be raised at the reunion.

definitions: To make something up or invent it as one goes on; to proceed guided only by imagination, instinct, and guesswork rather than by a careful plan.
synonyms: fly by the seat of one's pants

example: When the main speaker cancelled an hour before the event, the organiser had to improvise an entirely new programme on the spot.

definitions: Not intentional; not on purpose; not conscious.
Inattentive.

example: The disclosure was inadvertent — a document attached to the wrong email — but the damage it caused was very real.

definitions: Quickly proceeding to judgment and forceful in expression; decisive; forthright.
Intelligently analytical and concise.
Having the quality of incising, cutting, or penetrating, as with a sharp instrument; sharp; acute; sarcastic; biting.
Of or relating to the incisors.

example: Her incisive questioning cut through weeks of evasion and finally forced the committee to confront the central issue directly.

definitions: Skeptical, disbelieving, or unable to believe.
Expressing or indicative of incredulity.
Difficult to believe; incredible.

example: She stared at him with an incredulous expression, unable to believe that anyone could genuinely hold such an opinion.

definitions: Habitually lazy, procrastinating, or resistant to physical labor
Inducing laziness
Causing little or no physical pain; progressing slowly; inactive (of an ulcer, etc.)
Healing slowly

example: After years of indolent habits — sleeping late, avoiding effort — he found himself entirely unprepared when circumstances finally demanded otherwise.

definitions: Hard-working and persistent.

example: The industrious young engineer stayed late every night not because she was required to, but because she found the problems genuinely fascinating.

definitions: Beyond expression in words; unspeakable.
Forbidden to be uttered; taboo.

example: The view from the cliff at sunrise was ineffable — every attempt to describe it later in words felt hopelessly inadequate.

definitions: Not able to do something; not proficient; displaying incompetence.
Unfit; unsuitable.

example: His inept handling of the negotiation — making concessions before being asked and showing his hand too early — cost the company a far better deal.

definitions: Impossible to prevent or stop; inevitable.
Unable to be persuaded; relentless; unrelenting.
Adamant; severe.

example: The inexorable advance of the wildfire gave residents less than two hours to gather what they could and evacuate.

definitions: To introduce (something) as a reasoned conclusion; to conclude by reasoning or deduction, as from premises or evidence.
To lead to (something) as a consequence; to imply. (Now often considered incorrect, especially with a person as subject.)
To cause, inflict (something) upon or to someone.
To introduce (a subject) in speaking, writing etc.; to bring in.

example: From the empty desks, the darkened offices, and the silence, she could infer that everyone had already left for the day.

definitions: Naive and trusting.
Demonstrating childlike simplicity.
Unsophisticated; clumsy or obvious.
Unable to mask one's feelings.
Straightforward, candid, open, and frank.

example: He was so ingenuous that he took every promise at face value, never imagining that someone might have a hidden motive.

definitions: To hold in or hold back; to keep in check; restrain.
To recuse.

example: Fear of public embarrassment can inhibit learning — students afraid to be wrong rarely take the risks that lead to real understanding.

definitions: To cause to exist; to call into being.
Inborn; existing or having existed since birth.
Originating in, or derived from, the constitution of the intellect, as opposed to acquired from experience.
Instinctive; coming from instinct.
Joined by the base to the very tip of a filament.

example: Some musicians seem to possess an innate sense of rhythm that exists before any formal training begins.

definitions: One who or that which cannot be satiated.
Not satiable; incapable of being satisfied or appeased; very greedy

example: Her insatiable curiosity led her to read everything she could find on any subject that caught her interest, however briefly.

definitions: Producing harm in a stealthy, often gradual, manner.
Intending to entrap; alluring but harmful.
Treacherous.

example: The most insidious aspect of the misinformation campaign was how slowly and quietly it eroded public trust over several years.

definitions: Steadfast adherence to a strict moral or ethical code.
The state of being wholesome; unimpaired
The quality or condition of being complete; pure
With regards to data encryption, ensuring that information is not altered by unauthorized persons in a way that is not detectable by authorized users.
The ability of a system to provide timely warnings to users when they should not be used for navigation.

example: Her integrity was tested when she discovered the error in the data — reporting it would cost the company a major contract, but she reported it anyway.

definitions: An intermittent fever or disease.
Stopping and starting, occuring, or presenting at intervals; coming after a particular time span.
(specifically, of a body of water) Existing only for certain seasons; that is, being dry for part of the year.

example: The intermittent signal — strong for a few seconds, then gone entirely — made it impossible to maintain a reliable connection.

definitions: Fearless; bold; brave.

example: The intrepid correspondent spent three months embedded with aid workers in one of the world's most dangerous conflict zones.

definitions: One who has (especially parapsychological) intuition.
Spontaneous, without requiring conscious thought.
Easily understood or grasped by intuition.
Having a marked degree of intuition.

example: The interface was so intuitive that most users needed no instructions at all — they simply knew what to do next.

definitions: To cause someone to become accustomed to something (usually) unpleasant.
To take effect, to be operative.
To commit.

example: Years of working night shifts had inured her to the loneliness and fatigue that new hires found so difficult to endure.

definitions: To fix and settle after a long time; to entrench.
Firmly established from having been around for a long time; of long standing
(of a person) Having had a habit for a long time
Malignant; virulent; spiteful.

example: An inveterate traveller, he had visited over ninety countries and showed no sign of slowing down at seventy-two.

definitions: Characterized by or constituting (any kind of) irony.
Given to the use of irony; sarcastic.
Contrary or opposite to what may be expected.
Odd or coincidental; strange.

example: It was ironic that the man who had campaigned most loudly against development ended up selling his land to the very company he had opposed.

definitions: Having, characterized by, or done with good judgment or sound thinking.

example: A judicious selection of examples — neither too many nor too few — made the argument persuasive without feeling padded.

definitions: To place side by side, especially for contrast or comparison.

example: The exhibition juxtaposed photographs of the city taken in 1920 and 2020, making the transformation feel both vast and immediate.

definitions: To make cold, to sharpen.
Often with a prepositional phrase, or with to and an infinitive: showing a quick and ardent responsiveness or willingness; eager, enthusiastic, interested.
Fierce, intense, vehement.
Having a fine edge or point; sharp.
Acute of mind, having or expressing mental acuteness; penetrating, sharp.
Acrimonious, bitter, piercing.
Of cold, wind, etc.: cutting, penetrating, piercing, sharp.
Of prices, extremely low as to be competitive.
Marvelous.
Brave, courageous; audacious, bold.

example: She had a keen eye for inconsistency — the kind of small discrepancy in a document that others would scroll past without noticing.

definitions: Using as few words as possible; pithy and concise.

example: His laconic response — a single raised eyebrow and the word "interesting" — told her everything she needed to know about his opinion.

definitions: Worthy of being lauded; praiseworthy; commendable
Healthy; salubrious; having a disposition to promote healing

example: Their effort to provide meals to every child in the district over the summer, entirely through private fundraising, was genuinely laudable.

definitions: (forensics) The residue left by a person's finger that can be made visible by a process such as powder dusting; a latent fingerprint.
An underlying cause that can be inferred from statistical correlations; factor.
Anything that is latent.
Existing or present but concealed or inactive.
(of a virus) remaining in an inactive or hidden phase; dormant.
Lying dormant or hidden until circumstances are suitable for development or manifestation.

example: The latent talent the coach had spotted in him during that first informal session took years of patient work to fully develop.

definitions: Praise or glorification.
Hymn of praise.
(in the plural, also Lauds) A prayer service following matins.
To praise, to glorify

example: The report lauded the paramedics for their swift response, crediting their actions with saving three lives at the scene.

definitions: Sluggish, slow
Indifferent, apathetic

example: He felt lethargic for days after the illness passed — able to go through the motions but incapable of genuine focus.

definitions: Lightness of manner or speech, frivolity; lack of appropriate seriousness; inclination to make a joke of serious matters.
Lack of steadiness.
The state or quality of being light, buoyancy.
A lighthearted or frivolous act.

example: A touch of levity from the chair broke the tension after three hours of difficult debate, and the room visibly relaxed.

definitions: A lucid dream.
Clear; easily understood
Mentally rational; sane
Bright, luminous, translucent or transparent

example: Even in her nineties, her mind remained lucid — her recall sharp, her arguments clear, her wit entirely intact.

definitions: Producing a surplus; profitable.
Of a target: worth attacking; whose destruction is militarily useful.

example: What had begun as a weekend hobby became surprisingly lucrative once she started selling her work online.

definitions: Noble and generous in spirit.

example: Despite the years of rivalry and the bitterness of the campaign, he was magnanimous in victory, praising his opponent's long service.

definitions: Able to be hammered into thin sheets; capable of being extended or shaped by beating with a hammer, or by the pressure of rollers.
Flexible, liable to change.
(of an algorithm) in which an adversary can alter a ciphertext such that it decrypts to a related plaintext

example: Young children are far more linguistically malleable than adults — they absorb a second language without the same conscious effort.

definitions: An official or authoritative command; an order or injunction; a commission; a judicial precept.
The authority to do something, as granted to a politician by the electorate.
A papal rescript.
A period during which a government is in power.
To authorize
To make mandatory

example: The landslide result gave the new government a clear mandate to pursue the reforms it had promised throughout the campaign.

definitions: Something that is marginal.
A constituency won with a small margin.
Of, relating to, or located at or near a margin or edge; also figurative usages of location and margin (edge).
Determined by a small margin; having a salient characteristic determined by a small margin.
Pertaining to changes resulting from a unit increase in production or consumption of a good.

example: The improvement in test scores was marginal — just enough to claim progress, but not enough to suggest the programme was working.

definitions: To make lean.
Having little flesh; lean; thin.
Poor, deficient or inferior in amount, quality or extent
Of a set: such that, considered as a subset of a (usually larger) topological space, it is in a precise sense small or negligible.
Dry and harsh to the touch (e.g., as chalk).

example: The meager salary barely covered rent and groceries, leaving nothing at the end of each month and no prospect of saving.

definitions: Characterized by very precise, conscientious attention to details.
Timid, fearful, overly cautious.

example: He was meticulous in his preparation — rehearsing every answer, researching every panelist, and preparing for questions no one else anticipated.

definitions: To reduce, lessen, or decrease; to make less severe or easier to bear.
To downplay.

example: Planting trees along the roofline helped mitigate the heat island effect in the surrounding streets during summer months.

definitions: A modest, small, or trifling amount.

example: If he had shown even a modicum of remorse — any acknowledgment of what he had done — the outcome might have been different.

definitions: To regulate, adjust or adapt
To change the pitch, intensity or tone of one's voice or of a musical instrument
To vary the amplitude, frequency or phase of a carrier wave in proportion to the amplitude etc of a source wave (such as speech or music)
To move from one key or tonality to another, especially by using a chord progression

example: A skilled speaker modulates tone and pace — slowing for emphasis, quickening for energy — rather than speaking in a flat monotone throughout.

definitions: An unremarkable, ordinary human being.
(in various subcultures) A person considered to be "normal", part of the mainstream culture, outside the subculture, not part of the elite group.
The world outside fandom; the normal, mainstream world.
(satanism) A person not a Satanist.
Worldly, earthly, profane, vulgar as opposed to heavenly.
Pertaining to the Universe, cosmos or physical reality, as opposed to the spiritual world.
Ordinary; not new.
Tedious; repetitive and boring.

example: The most mundane details of daily life — grocery lists, bus schedules, small arguments — accumulated over decades into something that felt like a world.

definitions: In the form of a cloud or haze; hazy.
Vague or ill-defined.
Relating to a nebula or nebulae.

example: The plan was nebulous at best — full of aspirational language and vague timelines but almost entirely short on specifics.

definitions: Sinful, villainous, criminal, or wicked, especially when noteworthy or notorious for such characteristics.

example: The investigation eventually exposed the full extent of his nefarious scheme, which had defrauded hundreds of investors over ten years.

definitions: Able to be neglected, ignored or excluded from consideration; too small or unimportant to be of concern.

example: The added cost was negligible compared to the time it would save, so the committee approved the upgrade without debate.

definitions: Casually calm and relaxed.
Indifferent; unconcerned; behaving as if detached.

example: He shrugged with a nonchalant air, as though losing the account were a minor inconvenience rather than a crisis.

definitions: Newly made, formed or evolved; having no precedent; of recent origin; new.
Original, especially in an interesting way; new and striking; not of the typical or ordinary type.

example: The approach was entirely novel — no one in the field had thought to apply the technique this way, which is precisely why it worked.

definitions: A minor distinction.
Subtlety or fine detail.
To apply a nuance to; to change or redefine in a subtle way.

example: A good translator doesn't just convert words — they preserve the nuance of tone, implication, and cultural context that gives language its full meaning.

definitions: To harden; to obdure.
Stubbornly persistent, generally in wrongdoing; refusing to reform or repent.
Physically hardened, toughened.
Hardened against feeling; hard-hearted.

example: Despite overwhelming evidence that the plan was failing, he remained obdurate, refusing to hear any suggestion of adjustment.

definitions: A material object that physically exists.
A goal that is striven for.
(grammar) The objective case.
(grammar) a noun or pronoun in the objective case.
The lens or lenses of a camera, microscope, or other optical device closest to the object being examined.
Of or relating to a material object, actual existence or reality.
Not influenced by the emotions or prejudices.
Based on observed facts.
(grammar) Of, or relating to a noun or pronoun used as the object of a verb.
(grammar) Of, or relating to verbal conjugation that indicates the object (patient) of an action. (In linguistic descriptions of Tundra Nenets, among others.)

example: As a close friend of one of the parties, she knew she could not be fully objective and declined to serve on the review panel.

definitions: (of words, equipment, etc.) No longer in use; gone into disuse; disused or neglected (often by preference for something newer, which replaces the subject).
Imperfectly developed; not very distinct.

example: Within a decade, the technology that had seemed so advanced had become obsolete, replaced by systems that were cheaper and far more capable.

definitions: Stubbornly adhering to an opinion, purpose, or course, usually with implied unreasonableness; persistent.
Said of inanimate things not easily subdued or removed.

example: His obstinate refusal to seek medical attention turned what might have been a minor issue into a serious and prolonged one.

definitions: To make dark; overshadow
To deliberately make more confusing in order to conceal the truth.
To alter code while preserving its behavior but concealing its structure and intent.
Obfuscated; darkened; obscured.

example: The committee accused the agency of deliberately obfuscating the data — presenting figures in a way designed to mislead rather than inform.

definitions: An oblique line.
(grammar) The oblique case.
To deviate from a perpendicular line; to move in an oblique direction.
To march in a direction oblique to the line of the column or platoon; — formerly accomplished by oblique steps, now by direct steps, the men half-facing either to the right or left.
To slant (text, etc.) at an angle.
Not erect or perpendicular; neither parallel to, nor at right angles from, the base; slanting; inclined.
Not straightforward; indirect; obscure; hence, disingenuous; underhand; perverse; sinister.
Not direct in descent; not following the line of father and son; collateral.
Of leaves, having the base of the blade asymmetrical, with one side lower than the other.
Of branches or roots, growing at an angle that is neither vertical nor horizontal.
(grammar) Pertaining to the oblique case (non-nominative).

(grammar) Of speech or narration, indirect, employing the actual words of the speaker, but as related by a third person, having the first person in pronoun and verb converted into the third person, adverbs of present time into the past, etc.
Employing oblique motion, motion or progression in which one part (voice) stays on the same note while another ascends or descends.
A slashing action or motion, particularly:
A mark made by a slashing motion, particularly:
Something resembling such a mark, particularly:
The loose woody debris remaining from a slash, (particularly forestry) the trimmings left while preparing felled trees for removal.
Slash fiction.
A drink of something; a draft.
A piss: an act of urination.
A swampy area; a swamp.
A large quantity of watery food such as broth.
The period of a transitory breeze.
An interval of good weather.
The loose part of a rope; slack.

example: Rather than addressing the accusation directly, he gave an oblique response that seemed designed to leave maximum room for later interpretation.

definitions: Obedient; compliant with someone else's orders or wishes.
Excessively eager and attentive to please or to obey instructions; fawning, subservient, servile.
Of or pertaining to obsequies, funereal.

example: The obsequious assistant agreed with every opinion the director expressed, never offering a contrary view even when it might have helped.

definitions: To dull or reduce an emotion or a physical state.
Blunt; not sharp, pointed, or acute in form.
Intellectually dull or dim-witted.
Of sound, etc.: deadened, muffled, muted.
Indirect or circuitous.

example: He was being deliberately obtuse — pretending not to understand a point that every other person in the room had grasped immediately.

definitions: Of or pertaining to an omen or to omens; being or exhibiting an omen; significant.
Specifically, giving indication of a coming ill; being an evil omen

example: The board meeting had an ominous atmosphere from the start — no agenda circulated, no refreshments, and several faces no one recognised.

definitions: An area of darkness; a place or region with no light.
Something which is opaque rather than translucent.
To make, render (more) opaque.
Neither reflecting nor emitting light.
Allowing little light to pass through, not translucent or transparent.
Unclear, unintelligible, hard to get or explain the meaning of
Obtuse, stupid.
Describes a type for which higher-level callers have no knowledge of data values or their representations; all operations are carried out by the type's defined abstract operators.

example: The contract language was so opaque that three lawyers read it and arrived at three different conclusions about what it actually required.

definitions: Suitable for some particular purpose.
At a convenient or advantageous time.

example: She waited for the most opportune moment — when he was relaxed, in a good mood, and away from the office — before raising the subject.

definitions: To adorn; to honour.
Elaborately ornamented, often to excess.
Flashy, flowery or showy
Finely finished, as a style of composition.

example: The ballroom was almost overwhelmingly ornate — every surface gilded, every ceiling painted, every doorframe carved with figures and foliage.

definitions: Apparent, evident; meant for open display.
Appearing as such; being such in appearance; professed, supposed (rather than demonstrably true or real).

example: His ostensible reason for the visit was to deliver papers, but it quickly became clear he was there to gather information.

definitions: Of ostentation.
Intended to attract notice.
Of tawdry display; kitsch.

example: The party was so ostentatious — ice sculptures, a live orchestra, fireworks at midnight — that most guests felt uncomfortable rather than impressed.

definitions: A pattern, a way of doing something, especially a pattern of thought, a system of beliefs, a conceptual framework.
An example serving as the model for such a pattern.
A set of all forms which contain a common element, especially the set of all inflectional forms of a word or a particular grammatical category.

example: The discovery forced scientists to abandon the dominant paradigm and rebuild their theoretical framework almost entirely from scratch.

definitions: An apparently self-contradictory statement, which can only be true if it is false, and vice versa.
A counterintuitive conclusion or outcome.
A claim that two apparently contradictory ideas are true.
A thing involving contradictory yet interrelated elements that exist simultaneously and persist over time.
A person or thing having contradictory properties.
An unanswerable question or difficult puzzle, particularly one which leads to a deeper truth.
A statement which is difficult to believe, or which goes against general belief.
The use of counterintuitive or contradictory statements (paradoxes) in speech or writing.
A state in which one is logically compelled to contradict oneself.
The practice of giving instructions that are opposed to the therapist's actual intent, with the intention that the client will disobey or be unable to obey.

example: It is a genuine paradox that the countries with the most advanced medical systems often have among the least healthy populations.

definitions: Exhibiting parsimony; sparing in the expenditure of money; frugal to excess.
Using a minimal number of assumptions, steps, or conjectures.
Not conceding many goals.

example: He was so parsimonious that he drove forty minutes out of his way to buy petrol that was two cents cheaper.

definitions: An adherent to a party or faction.
A fervent, sometimes militant, supporter or proponent of a party, cause, faction, person, or idea.
A member of a band of detached light, irregular troops acting behind occupying enemy lines in the ways of harassment or sabotage; a guerrilla fighter.
The commander of a body of detached light troops engaged in making forays and harassing an enemy.
Serving as commander or member of a body of detached light troops.
Adherent to a party or faction; especially, having the character of blind, passionate, or unreasonable adherence to a party.
Devoted to or biased in support of a party, group, or cause.

example: The committee's findings were dismissed by critics as partisan — shaped by political loyalty rather than any independent analysis.

definitions: To act as a patron of; to defend, protect, or support.
To make oneself a customer of a business, especially a regular customer.
To assume a tone of unjustified superiority toward; to talk down to, to treat condescendingly.
To blame, to reproach.

example: She hated the way he patronized younger colleagues, explaining obvious concepts slowly and carefully as though they were incapable of understanding.

definitions: Having the appearance of deep, often melancholic, thinking.
Looking thoughtful, especially from sadness.

example: He sat at the window in a pensive mood, turning the problem over in his mind without yet being ready to speak about it.

definitions: Having or showing keenness of perception, insight, understanding, or intuition.

example: Her perceptive observation — that the client was answering questions they hadn't been asked while avoiding those they had — changed the direction of the inquiry.

definitions: Done only to fulfil a duty, or in a careless or indifferent manner; performed mechanically and as a thing of rote.

example: The inspection was perfunctory at best — a ten-minute walkthrough with no documentation, no tests, and no follow-up questions.

definitions: Causing much harm in a subtle way.
Causing death or injury; deadly.

example: The most pernicious effect of the rumour was not the immediate damage it caused but the slow erosion of trust that followed for years.

definitions: Manifested throughout; pervading, permeating, penetrating or affecting everything.

example: The smell of smoke was pervasive, reaching every corner of the building even though the fire was three floors away.

definitions: Of acute discernment; having keen insight; mentally perceptive.
Able to physically see clearly; quick-sighted; sharp-sighted.

example: A perspicacious reader would have noticed early on that the narrator was not entirely reliable, catching inconsistencies others missed.

definitions: Important with regard to (a subject or matter); relevant

example: Please keep your comments pertinent to the agenda — we have limited time and several items still to cover.

definitions: One who has a phlegmatic disposition.
Not easily excited to action or passion; calm; sluggish.
Abounding in phlegm.
Generating, causing, or full of phlegm.

example: She was so phlegmatic about the delays that her team couldn't tell whether she had a plan or had simply stopped caring about the outcome.

definitions: Concise and meaningful.
Of, like, or abounding in pith.

example: His pithy summary — "we spent a lot to learn a little" — captured the entire post-mortem in six words.

definitions: To calm; to bring peace to; to influence someone who was furious to the point that they become content or at least no longer irate.

example: The company tried to placate angry customers with discount vouchers, but most felt the apology itself was long overdue.

definitions: Seemingly or apparently valid, likely, or acceptable; conceivably true or likely
Obtaining approbation; specifically pleasing; apparently right; specious.
Worthy of being applauded; praiseworthy; commendable; ready.

example: The explanation was plausible enough to satisfy a casual observer, but it collapsed the moment anyone looked closely.
